actions.thought: It is the first hand of the tournament, and I am on the button in a 3-handed game. Ace-Six offsuit is a playable starting hand in this position. Raising is the standard aggressive play to seize initiative and potentially steal the blinds, rather than limping or folding.
I have a very weak hand, 7s 3s, in the Small Blind. The Button has opened to 2.5x the big blind. Being out of position with a weak suited three-gapper makes this an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250. While I only need to call 150 more to see a flop, 42o has extremely poor playability and realization of equity, especially when playing out of position for the remainder of the hand. In a 3-handed game, I should defend my big blind relatively wide, but 42o is at the very bottom of the range and not worth the investment. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I have a premium pair (Jacks) on the button. As I am first to act in this 3-handed game pre-flop, I want to open with a standard raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. Raising to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a standard sizing to extract value from worse hands while denying equity to overcards.

I am in the Small Blind with Q3o, which is a very weak hand to play out of position. The Button raised to 2.5bb. Q3o is near the bottom of my range and does not play well post-flop, especially against a standard opening range. I should fold and wait for better opportunities.

I hold pocket fours in the big blind against a button open. With 44, I have good equity against a standard opening range and am getting sufficient pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 400). Folding is too tight and 3-betting would inflate a pot with a mediocre hand, so I will call to see a flop and look for set-mining opportunities or a favorable board texture.

The board (8h, 9h, Ac) is very unfavorable for my pocket fours, as the Ace connects strongly with the opponent's button opening range. With no pair and no realistic draw other than a weak backdoor flush potential, my hand has very little equity. Betting into the aggressor here would be reckless. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's reaction.

The flop comes 8h 9h Ac, presenting an overcard to my pocket Jacks and a wet, coordinated board with flush and straight draws. Since the Big Blind defended pre-flop, their range includes plenty of Aces, flush draws, and straight draws. Betting here will often get called by better hands (any Ace) or strong draws, while mostly folding out worse hands. By checking behind, I exercise pot control with a medium-strength hand and can use my position to evaluate the turn. Additionally, holding the Jack of Hearts blocks some flush and straight combinations and gives me backdoor flush equity.

With three hearts on the board, my pocket fours are very vulnerable. Although I hold the four of hearts, giving me a marginal flush draw, my pair of fours is weak and likely behind even a random unpaired hand or a pair of threes/eights/nines. The opponent checked the flop, which could signify weakness or a trap. Betting here is suboptimal as it would bloat the pot with a weak hand that does not want to be raised. Checking is the most prudent path to control the pot size and see the river cheaply.

The 3h on the turn brings a third heart to the board, completing some flush draws, but I hold the Jack of Hearts, giving me a strong flush draw. The opponent checking twice suggests they likely don't have a made flush or a strong Ace that they want to build a pot with. While betting could serve as a semi-bluff or get value from worse hands, it also opens me up to a check-raise that could force me to fold my valuable equity. Checking behind exercises pot control, guarantees me a free card to hit my flush, and maintains showdown value if the river bricks.

The board shows four hearts (8h, 9h, 3h, Ah), creating a potential flush for any player holding a heart. With my 4h, I have an Ace-high flush (Ah, 9h, 8h, 4h, 3h). Given the passive check-check action on the flop and turn, it is highly likely that both players are playing the board, or at best, holding marginal cards. Betting is not advisable as I would likely only be called by a higher heart (Kh, Qh, Jh, Th), which would beat my flush. Checking is the optimal strategy to maximize showdown value and minimize risk, as it allows me to potentially split the pot if no one holds a superior flush.

The river brings a fourth heart and pairs the Ace. I hold the Jh, giving me a Jack-high flush. The opponent's three checks indicate they likely lack a premium flush (Kh, Qh) or a full house, and might be sitting on a weaker flush, trip Aces, or a marginal made hand. Checking behind would miss out on clear value. I will make a value bet of around half the pot to target hands like a naked Ace or a smaller heart that might find a call.

The board shows four hearts, creating a flush opportunity for anyone holding a heart. With the four of hearts, I have an Ace-high flush (Ah, 9h, 8h, 4h, 3h). Given the opponent's river raise after checking the previous streets, they could be betting for value with a better heart, bluffing, or simply overvaluing a pair of Aces. Folding a flush when receiving 4:1 pot odds would be mathematically incorrect. Raising is risky because my flush is near the bottom of the range and I would likely be called only by superior hands. Therefore, calling is the most balanced and profitable decision.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Nine offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative and utilize my positional advantage over the small and big blinds. After folding the first two hands, this is a good opportunity to enter the pot and define my range.

I hold pocket Aces in the small blind, which is the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Since the button has opened the action, I need to 3-bet to build the pot, take control of the hand, and discourage flat calling from the big blind. A 3-bet to 850 is a standard size that provides value and allows me to define the strength of my opponent's range early while playing out of position.

Facing an open raise from the Button and a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind, my QJo is severely outclassed. Hands like AQ, KQ, AJ, or high pocket pairs will frequently dominate me here. Folding is the only sensible play.

I opened from the button with Jack-Nine offsuit, but Flashlight in the small blind has responded with a 3-bet to 850. While I would have position post-flop, J9o is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by a standard 3-betting range (strong Aces, high pairs, and high Broadway cards). Given the stack depths and the strength shown by the re-raise, calling 600 more into a pot of 1200 is not a profitable play with this specific hand. It is better to fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

6-7 offsuit is a weak hand with limited value in a three-handed game, especially when acting first from the button. With 100 big blind stacks, it is strategically better to wait for higher equity cards to open the pot. Folding is the most prudent and disciplined play to preserve my stack for more favorable spots.

With action folded to me in the Small Blind, I have A4o. This is a reasonable hand to open aggressively to try and steal the Big Blind or take the initiative into the flop. Raising to 3x (300 chips) puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend with marginal holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Since Flashlight folded, this is a heads-up battle between the blinds. J9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a Small Blind open range, and I will have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds are favorable, as I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600 total (33% equity required). Call is the standard and most profitable play here.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a very dry board (Kc Ah 8s). As the pre-flop aggressor, this board hits my perceived range well. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200 chips) can extract value from worse hands like a King, an Eight, or floating hands, while allowing me to keep the pot manageable. If I face significant resistance, I can re-evaluate.

The flop of Ace, King, and Eight is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser (Pro), as their range is heavily concentrated with Aces and Kings. My hand, Jack-Nine offsuit, has completely missed this board and possesses no significant draws or showdown value. While the bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot) offers decent odds, calling with Jack-high in this spot is a losing play given how well the board texture hits the opponent's range. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

We are on the button (first to act in 3-handed play) with 64s, a marginal suited one-gapper. While it can sometimes be opened as a steal, folding is the most solid and lowest variance play here. We preserve our stack for better opportunities with stronger hands, especially since Flashlight showed aggression from the blinds in a recent hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 5d, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and Flashlight in the Big Blind. Even though it is only 50 more to call, playing such a weak hand out of position is statistically unprofitable in the long run. There is no reason to risk chips this early in the tournament with 2-5 offsuit.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button typically allows for a wide opening range, J2o lacks playability and is well outside the standard 40-50% opening range for this position. Given that both opponents have shown aggression in previous hands, I will play disciplined and fold this hand.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. With the button folding, I am heads-up against the big blind. Completing the small blind (calling the extra 50) is a standard, low-risk play that allows me to see a flop with a decent holding, maintaining a balanced approach early in the tournament.

With 5c 6h offsuit in the Big Blind and the Small Blind just limping, I have a weak unsuited connector. I have position post-flop, so checking to see a free flop is the standard and most profitable play. There is no need to bloat the pot with a weak hand pre-flop by raising.

The board of [2c, 4h, 3c] is highly coordinated and heavily favors the Big Blind's range, which includes many small cards, pairs, and straight combinations (like 5x or 6x). With Queen-Ten high and no draw, I have very little equity to justify a bluff bet into a range that hits this texture well. Betting here would likely lead to getting raised or called by better hands. Checking is the most standard, low-variance approach to minimize loss and potentially see a free turn card.

I flopped the nut straight (2-3-4-5-6) on a wet board with two clubs. Flashlight checked to me. I want to start building the pot and extracting value immediately from his range, which likely includes flush draws, smaller straight draws, Aces, or small pairs. A bet of 150 (3/4 pot) charges the draws appropriately while still getting value.

The flop texture of 2c-4h-3c is extremely favorable to the Big Blind's range, which contains many combinations of small cards and straight draws. My hand, Queen-Ten offsuit, has zero equity on this board with no pair and no draw. Pro's bet of 150 into a 350 pot represents strength, and calling with nothing but overcards would be a significant mistake, as I would likely be forced to fold on most turns. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance choice here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with suited cards (Qh, 5h), which is a good position to attempt to steal the blinds. Given that the action has folded to me and the pot contains the standard blinds, raising to 250 is an appropriate sizing to either win the pot immediately or see a flop in position.

Holding AKo in the Small Blind facing a Button raise is a prime spot for a 3-bet. AKo is a premium hand that is ahead of the Button's wide opening range. Raising builds the pot for value, folds out weaker hands, and puts maximum pressure on the opponent. Since we are out of position, sizing up to around 850 chips (about 3.4x the original raise) is standard to discourage the Big Blind from coming along and to give the Button bad pot odds to call with marginal hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 5s, which is a very weak starting hand. There has been an open-raise from the Button to 250 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 850. The pot is currently 1200 and it costs me 750 to call. 45o does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against two aggressive actions. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind after my button open, my holding of Queen-Five suited is too weak to continue against a tight-aggressive opponent. Although I have position, the 3-bet indicates a strong range, and calling 600 chips into the current pot with a marginal hand is not profitable. Folding is the disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

J9o is a marginal, unsuited hand. While we are on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand lacks the playability and equity to justify opening. Folding is the safest and most standard approach to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With the Button folding, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. 8h 5h is a solid suited hand that performs well in blind-vs-blind situations. I will raise to 3bb (300) to apply pressure on the Big Blind and take the initiative, which is generally more effective than just completing the small blind or folding a hand with this much potential equity.

Defending the big blind with Ten-Three suited is generally suboptimal as the hand has poor equity and playability against a standard raising range. The hand is easily dominated and playing out of position post-flop with such a weak holding is likely to lead to difficult decisions and chips loss. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic play here to preserve stack depth.

I am on the button with 4s 7c, which is a very weak hand. While playing on the button allows for a wide opening range in a 3-handed game, 47o is too low to profitably open or call, especially against aggressive opponents. I will fold and wait for a better hand.

With 3-4 offsuit, I have a very weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack, as this hand has minimal equity.

Pocket eights are a strong holding in a three-handed game. As the button, I should be opening a wide range, and pocket eights are well above my opening threshold. A raise to 250 is a standard sizing to build the pot and potentially take the blinds down or get value from weaker holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with an offsuit two-gapper, 6h 4s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Flashlight raised to 250 from the Button. Playing a junk hand out of position against a pre-flop raise is highly unprofitable. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I am in the Big Blind with KQo, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight has raised from the button, which is usually a wide range. To capitalize on my hand strength and take the initiative out of position, I will 3-bet to 850. This size is consistent with previous aggressive play seen at the table and puts significant pressure on the opener's range.

I opened the button with pocket eights and received a 3-bet from the big blind. Pocket eights are a strong holding, but they are vulnerable to overcards and often play best by seeing a flop in position. While 4-betting could represent strength, it risks bloating a pot against a range that may contain higher pairs or strong overcards. Calling is the most balanced approach, allowing me to utilize my position post-flop and evaluate the board texture.

I 3-bet pre-flop with KQo and was called by Flashlight. The flop comes 6-7-9 rainbow. This board texture is quite coordinated and significantly favors the range of a player who calls a 3-bet (which often includes medium pocket pairs and suited connectors) rather than the 3-bettor's range (which is heavier on high cards and high pairs). With only King high and no direct draw on this 'wet' board, I will check. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts. Betting into a board that favors the opponent's calling range is often risky and can lead to being forced out of the hand by a raise.

I hold a pair of eights on a 6-7-9 board, which is a highly connected and dangerous texture. My hand is vulnerable, but I also have significant equity with an open-ended straight draw (any 5 or 10 completes a straight). Since my opponent checked, they likely have a range that either missed or is playing trap/caution. By betting now, I can extract value from potential lower pairs or draws, protect my hand against overcards, and take control of the pot. A bet of approximately half-pot, 850, is a standard size to generate value while maintaining control.

The flop of 6-7-9 is highly coordinated and significantly favors the range of a player who calls a 3-bet, as it contains many medium pocket pairs and suited connectors. My King-Queen high has completely missed this board and possesses very little equity against a range that likely hit a piece of this texture. While the bet size from Flashlight is relatively small (about 1/3 pot), calling out of position with only overcards on such a 'wet' board is a losing play in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
Holding 92o on the button. This is a very weak, unplayable hand. The best move is to fold.

In a Blind vs Blind situation after the Button folds, T8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a post-flop play with range advantage, even though I will be out of position.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Six suited. Flash has opened from the small blind, which is a wide range move. Given that I have position and a reasonable holding, calling is the most balanced approach to realize my equity and see a flop. The pot odds are favorable to make the call.

I raised pre-flop from the Small Blind and the Big Blind called. The flop comes A-5-2 rainbow, which is an excellent board for me to continuation bet as I have a significant range advantage. My range includes all the strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ), while the Big Blind often has mid-range hands or lower pairs that will struggle to call on an Ace-high board. A small bet of 200 into the 600 pot should be sufficient to fold out most of the opponent's non-Ace hands while keeping my risk low.

I have top pair with an Ace on a very dry board. My kicker is weak, but the board texture is unlikely to have helped the opponent significantly unless they have a better Ace. Calling is the most balanced approach: it keeps my opponent's range wide, allows them to continue bluffing with air on the turn, and controls the pot size in case I am behind a better Ace.

The King on the turn is a scare card that generally favors my pre-flop raising range, but since the opponent called my continuation bet on an Ace-high flop, they likely have an Ace or a draw. With only Ten-high and no real equity to improve, I will check to see if the opponent checks behind, potentially giving me a free look at the river, or to fold if they bet, as bluffing into a range that called an Ace-high flop is risky.

Flash has checked the turn after leading the flop. With top pair, weak kicker, checking behind is the optimal play. Betting now would only likely be called by hands that have me beat (Kings or better Aces) or cause the opponent to fold hands I am currently ahead of. By checking, I control the pot size and allow the opponent to potentially barrel again on the river with air, or I get a free card to reach showdown.

On the river, the board is A-5-2-K-6. I have Ten-high. While I represented the Ace with my flop continuation bet, my check on the turn likely signaled a lack of strength to my opponent. Flashlight called the flop bet, suggesting they have at least a small pair or a weak Ace. Since the river 6 did not improve my hand or provide a credible card to bluff on after checking the turn, I will check and likely fold if bet into, or hope for a check-back to see if my Ten-high can miraculously win, though it is highly unlikely.

I hold top pair with a weak kicker, which has improved to two pair, Aces and Sixes. Flash checked the river after checking the turn, suggesting they do not have a strong hand or have given up on a draw. Betting would likely fold out hands I beat and get called by hands that beat me (better Aces), so checking behind is the most profitable way to realize showdown value.

I am on the button with 7d 4c, which is a very weak hand. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, 74 offsuit has very little equity if called or raised. Both Flashlight and Pro have shown aggression in previous hands, so folding this hand is the most disciplined and prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. There is no strategic value in playing this hand out of position. Folding is the correct decision to avoid potentially losing more chips in a marginal spot.

I am holding King-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand in a three-handed game. With no initiative, this hand has poor playability and is easily dominated by any King or Ace. The most disciplined and profitable strategy is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Queen-deuce offsuit is one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite only needing to call 50 to see the flop from the small blind, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and is often dominated. Folding is the best choice here to preserve chips.

T5o is a very weak hand with almost no playability or high card strength. From the button, it is a clear fold. There is no need to unnecessarily risk chips by attempting to steal the blinds with such poor hole cards.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 3c. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. While 63s is suited, it is a very weak hand to play out of position. Completing the blind for 50 chips is an option, but against a competent opponent, I will likely struggle to realize my equity post-flop. I choose to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with K2o. While the King has some high card value, K2o is a weak hand to play from the button, especially since the blinds have been relatively active and aggressive. Flashlight and Pro both have larger stacks than I do. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I hold pocket Aces, the best possible starting hand. With the button having folded, I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. Raising here is the standard, aggressive play to build the pot and exert pressure, allowing me to take control of the hand.
4d 6c is a weak offsuit one-gapper. Against a 3x raise from the small blind, this hand is a clear fold. I have no strong equity or playability post-flop to justify calling the raise.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 9d Jc. While this is not a premium hand, being on the button provides a significant positional advantage, and opening with a wider range is standard strategy in short-handed play to capture the blinds. I will open with a raise to 250 to apply pressure and test the opponents' resistance.

We are in the Small Blind with an offsuit connector (87o) facing a Button raise. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressive opener is generally unprofitable, and it's too weak to justify a 3-bet bluff. The best and most standard play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Flashlight opened to 250 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more into a total pot that will be 550. This gives me excellent pot odds (27%) to see a flop. Queen-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard button opening range, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I am first to act on a King-high board after calling a raise from the button. My hand, Queen-Ten offsuit, completely missed the flop. While I have two overcards to the middle cards (8 and 4), I have no made hand and no strong draw. This board significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many King-X combinations. My standard play here is to check and evaluate Flashlight's action. Donk betting into the aggressor on this texture would not be a sound strategy with my current holding.

The board is relatively dry (King-high, no immediate flush/straight draws), and I am in position after opening the pot. My opponent checked to me. A continuation bet (C-bet) is the standard strategic play here to represent the King and fold out equity from weak hands that missed this board. Betting around 35-40% of the pot (200 chips) is sufficient to test the opponent's strength without over-committing to a hand with only Jack-high.

I defended my big blind with Queen-Ten offsuit against a button raise, which was a standard play given the pot odds. However, the flop (Kh 4h 8c) is unfavorable for my hand. I have no pair, no draw, and I am facing a continuation bet from Flashlight. While the bet size is relatively small (200 into 550), my hand has very little equity against his range, which contains many Kings and pocket pairs. I have no reason to believe I can successfully bluff him out of position or that I have the best hand. The most disciplined play here is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.
53o is a very weak unsuited hand. Even from the Button, it is unprofitable to play in an unopened pot. Folding is the only sensible decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited (Tc 8c). Since the button folded, I am in a head-up situation with the Big Blind (Flashlight). T8s is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Rather than just completing the blind, I will raise to 300 to take initiative and attempt to win the pot immediately or set up a post-flop play with a hand that has good equity and playability.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 4h. This is a very weak starting hand with poor equity, and calling a 300 raise out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash is not profitable. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a speculative low-suited hand. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While the button offers a positional advantage, 9-4 offsuit does not play well post-flop and is easily dominated. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am currently the shortest stack (though still deep at 77 BBs), there is no reason to open-raise or call with such a weak hand. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Flash folded from the button, leaving me in the small blind against the big blind. Jc Td is a strong enough holding for a steal attempt in a heads-up scenario from the small blind. Raising here allows me to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or continue with position if called. A raise to 300 is a standard size to put pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind. My hand, 2s 3h, is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It is unsuited, disconnected, and has no high-card value. Although the pot odds might seem tempting, playing such a weak hand out of position (if not heads-up against the SB, but here I have position on the SB but still a completely unplayable hand) is a losing strategy. Folding is the only correct decision.

9-10 offsuit is a marginal hand that does not warrant an investment pre-flop. With 100 to call, folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities and avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop.
QJo is a strong hand in a blind vs. blind scenario. With the Button folding, raising from the Small Blind puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take the initiative into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six of clubs. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300 (a 3x open). I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 600. Q6s is a reasonable hand to defend with, especially since I will have position on the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds (calling 200 to win 400) are favorable, and the hand has decent playability post-flop.

On an Ace-high dry flop, a small continuation bet is highly profitable as the board strongly favors my pre-flop raising range. I will bet 1/3 pot (200) to fold out my opponent's missed broadways, underpairs, and weak floats, representing top pair or better. With QJ high and no immediate draw, taking down the pot now is the best outcome.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six of clubs on an Ace-Eight-Five rainbow board. The Small Blind (Pro), who was the pre-flop aggressor, has lead out with a continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot (33% pot). While the pot odds are tempting (200 to win 800), my hand has very little equity. I have no pair and only a runner-runner flush draw. The Ace-high board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. In a similar situation in Hand #4, I folded to the same sizing on an Ace-King-Eight board. Continuing here with just Queen-high against a range-betting opponent is unlikely to be profitable in the long run, so I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K5o is a weak hand even on the Button. While a steal attempt could sometimes work, it's generally a fold in an early stage of the tournament when our stack is healthy. We don't want to get involved with marginal holdings out of position post-flop if the blinds defend.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-2 suited. While the pot odds to call are 3:1, 7-2 is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Playing this hand out of position against a chip leader (Flashlight) who has shown aggression in previous hands is likely to lead to larger losses on future streets. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

In a three-handed game, standard opening ranges from the button are significantly wider than in full-ring games. King-Five offsuit is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing for opening raises.

I am in the small blind with King-Ten offsuit against a button open. This hand is well within my calling range for defending the small blind, despite being out of position post-flop. While 3-betting is an option, it is risky OOP with this holding, so calling is the most balanced approach to see a flop.

I am dealt a weak offsuit hand (Th 7d) in the Big Blind. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, playing this hand out of position multiway is unprofitable. Folding is the optimal decision.

The board is paired with 6c-6s-Qd, which is relatively dry but makes it hard to bet as a bluff. My hand, King-Ten offsuit, has minimal equity and does not interact well with this texture. Checking to the original aggressor (the button) is the standard and most prudent play in this position to control the pot size, see if they continuation bet, and reassess on the turn.

On a dry, paired board of 6c 6s Qd, I have a clear range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight checked to me, which is standard for a caller out of position. While my Kh 5c has not connected, it is a good candidate for a continuation bet to fold out hands like small pocket pairs or lower high cards that missed. A bet of roughly 43% of the pot (300 chips) should be effective in this spot to take down the pot immediately or define Flashlight's hand.

Facing a continuation bet on a dry, paired board (6c-6s-Qd) while holding King-Ten high, I have very little equity. My hand has virtually no showdown value against the button's betting range, which often includes pocket pairs, broadway cards, or a six. Calling 300 chips into a 1000 chip pot out of position without a pair or strong draw is mathematically and strategically unfavorable. It is better to fold now and preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am in the button position with 5h 6d, which is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. Opening with such a hand is generally unprofitable and unnecessary given the current stack sizes. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal strategy to preserve my chip stack and wait for a stronger holding.

With 87 offsuit in the Small Blind, playing out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. The hand is too weak to raise for value and lacks the playability to limp. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.
Q3o is a very weak offsuit hand, and even from the Button in a 3-handed game, it falls well outside of a standard open-raising range. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 83s, which is a marginal but playable suited connector. The Button (Pro) has already folded. I need to call 50 more to see a flop against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Given the pot odds and the fact that it's a heads-up situation from the blinds, calling to see a flop is a standard play to realize equity, though raising is also an option. I'll choose to call here and see how the flop develops.
I am in the Big Blind with a weak, unsuited hand (4c-Td). Since no one has raised, checking is the correct action to see the flop for free. Raising is not advisable given the poor hand strength.

I am in the Small Blind and checked to see the flop in a limped pot with 83s. The flop is 7d Ad 7c, which is a very dry and paired board. I have no pair and no real draw. Flashlight, the Big Blind, also checked pre-flop. In a limped pot, it's often best to check and see if the opponent wants to bet or give a free card. Since I'm out of position and have very little equity on this board, I will check to see how Flashlight reacts.

I am in the Big Blind with 10-high on a board of 7d-Ad-7c. My hand has no showdown value and minimal equity. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a potentially trapped Ace or a made Seven. Checking back is the prudent choice to keep the pot small and avoid unnecessary risk.

I have improved to two pair (8s and 7s with an Ace kicker) on the turn. Since the flop was checked around in a limped pot, it is unlikely that Flashlight has a very strong hand like an Ace or a 7, though they could be slow-playing. By betting small here (100 into a 200 pot), I can extract value from lower pocket pairs or potential straight/flush draws while keeping the pot manageable if I am behind.

I have 10-high on a board of 7d-Ad-7c-8h. I have no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. The opponent (Flash) has shown strength by betting the turn after checking the flop, suggesting they may have hit a pair (like an 8) or have an Ace. With no equity and being out of position, calling is a losing play. I will fold and save my chips for a better opportunity.

Q3o is a very weak hand, even in a 3-handed game from the button. There is no strategic reason to enter the pot or attempt a steal with this hand given the stack depths and the tendency of the blinds to defend. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. This gives me a heads-up situation against the big blind. With Ace-Eight offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise and apply pressure to the big blind to try and win the pot pre-flop or take the lead in the hand.

My hand is Jack-Four offsuit, which is extremely weak and disconnected. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, calling or trying to re-steal with such a marginal hand is unprofitable. Folding is the best decision.

Pocket fives is a strong hand to open from the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard 3x open, providing a good balance of value and aggression to see a flop in position.

Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, Qs8s is a marginal hand. Calling leaves me out of position against the Button, and the Big Blind could squeeze behind me. This hand is a bit too weak to play as a standard 3-bet bluff, so folding is the safest and most optimal play here.

I am in the Big Blind with King of Spades and Four of Clubs (K4o). The player on the Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. K4o is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially against a 3x open. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, K4o lacks playability and is frequently dominated by better Kings or strong high-card hands in a button-opening range. I have seen Flashlight be aggressive in previous hands, and I would prefer to wait for a hand with better post-flop potential or better equity to commit chips as the shortest stack at the table.

I am on the Button with 4h Ts, which is a very weak and unconnected offsuit hand. Even though I have position, this hand is not suitable for opening or attempting a steal. Folding to conserve chips is the optimal standard play.

J5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the small blind. Since the button folded, I could potentially raise to steal, but Flashlight has shown aggression in the big blind previously. Calling is also unattractive as I would be out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play.

I am on the Button with Ace-Trey offsuit. In a 3-handed game, any Ace is a strong starting hand, especially from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I hold 8-4 offsuit in the small blind, which is a very weak hand. Given I am out of position against an opening raise from the button, there is no mathematical justification to call or defend this hand. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision to preserve my stack.

Flash opened to 3bb from the Button. While A2o has an Ace blocker, it plays very poorly post-flop out of position and is generally too weak to defend by calling against a 3bb open. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but there are much better candidates like suited wheel aces or suited connectors. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

With 8c-5d, I have a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially in a three-handed game. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack size while waiting for a more advantageous opportunity to enter a pot from the button position.

I am in the small blind with T6o after the button folded. T6o is a very weak offsuit hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against the big blind. The standard and most profitable decision here is to fold and save my chips.

Seven-Deuce suited is an extremely weak hand. Although we are on the Button, there is virtually no value in playing this hand unless we want to run an unwarranted bluff, which has negative expected value. Folding is the standard and most mathematically sound choice here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Since the Button has folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind. J8o is a decent hand to open-raise with in a blind-vs-blind situation to try and take down the pot pre-flop. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand to defend from the big blind against a small blind raise. Given that the small blind's opening range is likely wide, I have a significant equity advantage. I will call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable while playing in position to the aggressor pre-flop, although out of position post-flop.

As the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board, I have a significant range advantage. My Jack-high has no showdown value, so I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to represent the Ace and force a fold from hands that missed or small pocket pairs.

I have top pair with an Ace and a solid kicker on a dry, rainbow board. This is a very strong hand in this spot, and I am almost certainly ahead of the opponent's range. Raising could scare off potential bluffs or weaker hands, while calling keeps the opponent in the hand and allows me to control the pot size while extracting value later if I remain ahead.

On this Ace-high board, my continuation bet on the flop was called by the Big Blind. The Turn 6s is relatively neutral but doesn't improve my Jack-high hand. Since my opponent called the flop, they likely have a piece of the board (an Ace, a Nine, or a diamond draw). Continuing to bluff into a range that contains many Aces is risky. I will check here to see how they respond; if they check back, I may consider a river bluff depending on the card, but if they bet, I will likely fold as my hand has no showdown value.

With top pair and a solid kicker on a dry, rainbow board, I am likely ahead of the opponent's range. Since the opponent checked the turn, they are likely either holding a weaker hand, a marginal pair, or a draw they are not ready to commit with. I should bet for value to extract chips from weaker Aces or second pairs, while also charging any potential draws that might be looking to see a cheap river card.

My continuation bet on the flop was called by the Big Blind, indicating they likely have a piece of this Ace-high board or a draw. When I checked the turn, the opponent led out with a 400 bet. I currently hold Jack-high with no significant draws or showdown value. While the bet is small (1/3 pot), calling would require me to either hit a miracle on the river or execute a large bluff on a board that heavily favors the opponent's calling range (which likely includes many Aces, nines, or diamond draws). Given my lack of a hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Ten-Six suited. This is a standard opening hand for this position to put pressure on the blinds. A 3x raise is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the pot early or see a flop with position if called.

Q4 offsuit is a very weak hand, especially out of position in the small blind against a button raise. The pot odds do not justify a call, and this is not a suitable hand to 3-bet bluff with. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind with 2s 8c offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the button, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising with such a poor holding. The correct decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand that demands an aggressive approach, especially in position on the button. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard to build value early and potentially narrow the field to heads-up, maximizing my equity with a strong pocket pair.

52s is a very weak hand, even suited. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, playing this out of position from the Small Blind is massively unprofitable. The gap is too large to reliably make straights, and a 5-high flush draw or pair of 5s/2s will frequently be dominated. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with 10h 4h. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. In 3-handed play, Button opening ranges are typically wide. While 10-4 suited is a marginal hand, the pot odds (better than 2:1) and the fact that it is suited make it a reasonable hand to defend the blind with. I will call to see a flop and play post-flop based on board texture.

On this 9d-3h-4d flop, I have flopped bottom pair with a ten kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw. In a heads-up pot against the Button raiser, the standard play from the Big Blind is to check to the aggressor. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out for value. By checking, I can see how Flashlight proceeds. If they bet a standard amount, I plan to call at least one street to see if I improve or if the board texture remains favorable for my pair of fours.

I have an overpair on a relatively dry board that features a potential flush draw. A continuation bet is necessary to extract value from hands like 9x or a weak 4x, and to protect my equity against opponents holding diamond draws or overcards that might improve. A bet of 350, just over half the pot, is a standard size to build the pot and dictate the tempo.

I have bottom pair (fours) with a Ten kicker and a backdoor heart flush draw on a board of 9d-3h-4d. Flashlight's continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650 offers me pot odds of approximately 2.8:1, meaning I need about 26% equity to justify a call. Given that Flashlight can be betting with a wide range of hands including overcards, straight draws, or air, my bottom pair is currently ahead of a portion of their range and has decent potential to improve on the turn. Folding would be too conservative here, and raising would turn a hand with showdown value into a bluff. Therefore, calling is the most appropriate action to see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the 8h has improved my hand significantly. I still hold bottom pair (fours), but I have now picked up a flush draw to go along with it. In this heads-up pot against Flashlight, I am out of position. Since I was the caller on the flop, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Flashlight reacts to the turn card. If they bet, my hand now has enough equity (pair + flush draw) to easily call another bet. If they check behind, I get a free card to potentially hit my flush or improve to two pair/trips on the river. Leading out here would likely only be called by better hands and would force me to play a larger pot out of position.

I have an overpair on a turn that remains relatively dry despite the added flush draw potential. My opponent checked, indicating they are likely still chasing, holding a weak pair like a 9 or 4, or playing a draw cautiously. By betting now, I extract value from these inferior hands and protect my hand against potential straight or flush draws on the river. A bet of 700, which is roughly half the pot, is large enough to charge draws but not so large that I fold out hands I currently beat.

On the turn, the 8h has significantly improved my hand. I now have bottom pair (fours) plus a flush draw (9 outs to a heart). The board is 9d-3h-4d-8h. Flashlight has bet 700 into a pot of 1350, making the total pot 2050. I need to call 700, which gives me pot odds of approximately 25.4%. With 9 outs for a flush, 3 outs for two pair (tens), and 2 outs for trips (fours), I have roughly 14 outs to improve to a very strong hand, giving me about 30% equity. This equity alone justifies a call based on pot odds, and I also have implied odds if I hit my flush on the river. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands like top pair or overpairs, so calling to realize my equity out of position is the most profitable play.

The river is the 7c, and I have failed to improve my hand beyond the pair of fours I flopped. The heart flush draw I picked up on the turn has missed. On this board (9d-3h-4d-8h-7c), a pair of fours is extremely weak, especially after Flashlight has shown aggression on both the flop and the turn. There are many hands in a Button opening range that beat me, including any 9, any 8, any 7, or pocket pairs. Since I have some very limited showdown value but cannot expect to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call a bet, the most standard and disciplined play is to check. If Flashlight bets, I will likely have to fold as my hand is now just a missed draw with bottom pair.

The river 7c completed a possible straight (5-6-7-8-9) and gave me no improvement to my pocket queens. The board is now extremely coordinated, making it very likely that the opponent has connected with the board in some way—either through a straight, two pair, or at least a pair of 9s, 8s, or 7s, all of which beat my pocket queens. Betting at this point is purely value-cutting myself; I will likely only get called by hands that have me beat, and worse hands that might have been calling with draws (like missed diamonds) will simply fold. Checking back is the standard play to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with J8s. J8 suited is a marginal but playable hand, and strong enough to open-raise from the button to steal the blinds or play post-flop in position. I'll make a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Ten-Six is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, and with the Big Blind still to act behind me, calling or raising here is unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten suited. This is a very strong hand to defend against a standard Button open raise. Given the pot odds and my position, calling is the most balanced and profitable play. I will see a flop and evaluate based on how it interacts with my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-Queen-Ten board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking to the aggressor is the standard play. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many turn cards. I want to keep the pot controlled and see what action Pro takes.

The A-Q-T flop connects very heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, meaning they will frequently have an Ace, Queen, Ten, or a strong draw like K-J. While I have a gutshot straight draw with my J-8, it's to the lower end of the straight (any King would beat me if a 9 comes). Since my draw is weak and I have very little fold equity on such a coordinated Broadway board, checking behind to take a free card and keep the pot small is the safest and most optimal play.

We are on the river after the turn was checked through by both players. With an Ace and a Queen on the board, my middle pair of Tens is a marginal hand with some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet against an opponent who has shown passivity. Checking is the optimal play to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown. If the opponent bets, I will re-evaluate, but for now, checking allows me to capitalize on my positional advantage as the last acting player if they check, or simply take the free showdown.

Flashlight has checked again, giving me the opportunity to either bluff or take a free card. Since my hand has very little equity (only a weak gutshot to a non-nut straight) and the broadway-heavy board hits the Big Blind's defending range well, a delayed c-bet bluff might not generate enough fold equity. They could easily be trapping with a weak Ace or planning to call with a Ten. Checking behind ensures pot control and allows me to see the river for free, preserving my stack with a marginal holding.

With Middle Pair on a board featuring an Ace and a Queen, my hand is extremely vulnerable. The opponent showed passivity by checking behind on both the flop and the turn, which suggests they either have a marginal hand, a missed draw, or are trap-checking with a strong hand. Betting here is unlikely to induce calls from worse hands while risking a raise from better ones. Checking is the standard play to reach a cheap showdown and potentially induce a bluff from the opponent if they missed their draws.

With Jack-high and no showdown value, my only way to win this pot is by bluffing. Flashlight has checked all three streets, showing extreme weakness and capping their range to missed draws (like K-J or K-9), underpairs, or very marginal pairs (like a Ten or Seven) that want to get to a cheap showdown. My line of checking the flop and turn can easily represent a pot-controlled marginal made hand like a weak Queen, or a slow-played Ace that now wants to extract value on the river. A bet of roughly half to two-thirds pot should generate enough fold equity against better high-card hands and weak pairs.

My opponent checked behind on both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak range or a very disguised trap. By betting 350 into a 900 pot on the river, the opponent is likely testing the waters with a marginal hand or a missed draw, or perhaps making a small value bet with a hand like a Queen or a weak Ace. Given the pot odds and the passivity shown by the opponent throughout the hand, my pair of Tens has sufficient showdown value to justify a call. Folding would be too exploitable against potential bluffs, and raising is unnecessary as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands.

I am on the button with 9d 7d, which is a strong suited connector. Both opponents have larger stacks, but I have a playable hand with post-flop potential. Usually, raising from the button is standard to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. However, given the 'to call' is only 100, I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately.

6h3s is an extremely weak hand with no playability. Calling out of position against a button raise is negative expected value. Given the tournament structure and stack depths, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, 5h 9h is a bit too wide to defend from the Big Blind, even closing the action. A suited four-gapper plays poorly out of position post-flop, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this late position. With the blinds at 50/100 and the pot currently at 150, I want to take the initiative and maximize my advantage. Raising to 300 is a standard, disciplined open-raise size to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds or force my opponents into a defensive position.

A2o is a weak holding to play from the small blind against a 3x button open. Being out of position post-flop with a weak ace and no kicker puts us in a tricky spot, especially with the Big Blind still left to act behind. The standard and safest play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Flashlight, the chip leader on the Button, has raised to 300 (3x). There is no reason to defend such a poor hand against a strong opponent, especially with another player still to act in later hands. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I have 2c 6h on the button. This is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em and completely unplayable from any position, even for a steal. The correct action is an immediate fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Against a big stack like Flashlight, it is better to fold such a weak hand than to play a marginal pot out of position.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives (5c 5h). In short-handed play, pocket pairs are relatively strong. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to exert pressure and gain initiative, taking advantage of my position.

KTo is a solid holding in the small blind against a button open. While 3-betting is an option, Flash has been aggressive, and flatting keeps the pot size manageable while allowing me to evaluate the flop out of position. I am effectively getting good odds to call and see if I hit top pair or better.
I have 85 offsuit in the big blind facing a button raise and a small blind call. This is a very weak hand with poor equity and terrible playability post-flop, making it an easy fold.

The flop is heavily skewed toward an Ace, which is squarely in the range of a button raiser. My holding, King-high, has effectively zero showdown value and no meaningful drawing equity on this board texture. Checking is the standard play here out of position to control the pot size and see if the opponent, who has the initiative, continues with a continuation bet or decides to check back. If Flash bets, I will likely fold given the strength of the board for his range.

I have flopped a set of fives (5c 5h on 5d 4c Ah), which is an exceptionally strong hand. Since Flashlight checked to me, I want to start building the pot and extract value from any Ax hands or potential draws. A bet of 350 (half pot) is a standard continuation bet that keeps their range wide while ensuring I get paid for my monster hand.

The flop is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser given the Ace on board. With King-high and no drawing equity, my hand has no showdown value. As indicated in my previous strategic notes, I decided that the correct move against a continuation bet on this texture is to fold. Calling here would be a mistake as I would be betting into an extremely strong range with no path to improve.
5s 9h is a weak, unsuited hand with no real strength or playability. Even on the button, there is no justification for entering the pot with this holding. I will fold to conserve my stack.

With A4s in the small blind and the button folded, I have a strong starting hand for a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take the initiative. A 3x raise to 300 is standard and helps fold out random junk while building a pot for my suited ace.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Since I have position on the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand and my hand is well ahead of a typical SB opening range, calling is a very standard and effective play. This allows me to see a flop and use my positional advantage without bloating the pot prematurely against a larger stack.

I flopped bottom two pair (Aces and Fours) on a relatively wet board with a flush draw and straight draws possible. Since I am out of position, I need to bet for value and protection, charging worse Aces, Queens, and any club or straight draws. A bet of about two-thirds of the pot accomplishes this while building the pot for my strong holding.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. In a blind-vs-blind battle, this hand is too strong to fold to a single continuation bet, as the Small Blind's (Pro) range includes many bluffs, draws like KJ/KT, or smaller pairs. While the Ace is a concern, calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and see how the turn develops without overcommitting against a potentially better hand.

I have top and bottom pair (Aces and Fours) on a safe turn card (6h). My opponent's flop call indicates they likely hold a weaker Ace, a Queen, or a club flush draw. The 6h doesn't complete any obvious draws, so my two pair is still very strong. I need to continue betting for value and to deny equity to any club or straight draws. A bet of about 70% of the pot charges worse hands and sets up a favorable stack-to-pot ratio for the river.

The Small Blind is firing a second, large barrel (about 70% of the pot) on a board featuring an Ace, which is very much in their perceived pre-flop raising range. While I have middle pair with a strong kicker (Queens with King), the sizing of this turn bet suggests a high level of confidence or a semi-bluff with significant equity. Calling here would leave me with a difficult decision on the river and commit nearly 20% of my remaining stack to a hand that is likely behind an Ace. I will choose to fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.
84s is a very weak suited three-gapper. Even from the button, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call. Folding is the standard and correct play.

With the Button folding, the action is on me in the Small Blind. Holding J7o, I have a marginal hand but am getting 3:1 pot odds to call the remaining 50 chips to see a flop against Flashlight in the Big Blind. While J7o isn't strong enough for a frequent raise out of position, completing the blind is a standard play here to see if I can hit a favorable board cheaply.
My opponent completed the small blind, effectively limping. Holding a weak hand like 4-9 offsuit, there is no value in raising here. I will check to see the flop for free.

On a board of 7h-5h-Ad, I have flopped middle pair (sevens) with a Jack kicker. This is a reasonable hand in a limped pot, but the Ace is a scary card that often hits the Big Blind's range. I will check to see how Flashlight reacts; if they bet small, I will likely call, but checking allows me to control the pot size and see if they have an Ace or a draw.

I am in the big blind with 9-high on an Ace-high, disconnected board. I have no pair, no strong draw (only a backdoor flush possibility), and no showdown value. Checking is the standard play here to see the turn for free and avoid inflating a pot I am unlikely to win against any range that includes an Ace or even a pair of 5s or 7s.

On the turn, the board is 7h-5h-Ad-4d. I still have middle pair (sevens). The turn card (4d) doesn't change much for my hand, but it does introduce some straight possibilities (3-6) and a second flush draw. Since the flop went check-check, it's less likely either of us has a strong Ace, but I still want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand. Checking again is the most balanced play to see what my opponent does and potentially get to a cheap showdown.

I have checked the flop and the turn has come the 4 of diamonds, giving me a pair of 4s. My opponent, Flash, checked again on the turn. While I now have a pair, it is a very weak one with a 9-kicker. Betting would likely only get called by hands that have me beat (like a 5, 7, or an Ace) or fold out hands that I am currently beating. Checking behind is the best way to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown. I have no reason to build a pot with such a marginal holding.

On the river, the board has paired the Ace, making my hand two pair (Sevens and Aces) with a Jack kicker. Since the action went check-check on both the flop and the turn, it is highly unlikely that Flashlight has an Ace or a strong hand. I have significant showdown value here. While I could consider a small value bet, checking is a safer way to reach showdown or potentially induce a bluff from missed draws (like the heart or diamond draws). Given the small pot and the passive nature of the hand so far, I will check and plan to call a reasonably sized bet.

I am holding a pair of 4s with a 9-kicker on an Ace-high board with two Aces present. My opponent, Flash, has checked to me on the river. My hand is extremely marginal and has very little showdown value against a range that includes any Ace, 5, or 7. Betting would not likely induce calls from worse hands, and I would be putting money into the pot with a hand that is likely beaten. Checking behind is the optimal play to reach a cheap showdown.

I am on the button with J8o in a 3-handed game. In this position, I should be opening a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of my positional advantage for the rest of the hand. J8o is a standard opening hand in a 3-handed environment. I will raise to 300 to attempt to win the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am holding 4s 2s in the small blind, which is an extremely weak hand with poor playability. Calling a 3x raise out of position with such a disconnected low-suited connector is a negative expected value (-EV) play. I should conserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

Defending the big blind with a suited one-gapper (T8s) against a button open is standard. Getting decent odds to see a flop, and the hand has good post-flop playability. I'll call and evaluate on the flop.

On this paired, low board (5c 7h 5d), I don't have a direct draw or made hand with my Td 8d, just a backdoor flush draw and some overcards. As the out-of-position pre-flop caller, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser and evaluate their continuation bet.

The flop is 5c-7h-5d, a dry, paired board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a good board to continuation bet (C-bet) because it is difficult for the Big Blind to have connected with it unless they have a 5 or a 7. A bet of roughly half the pot should be enough to fold out most of the opponent's non-pairing hands that have equity against my Jack-high. If I am called, I will likely check behind on many turn cards unless I improve or pick up a strong draw.

Facing a continuation bet of nearly half the pot on a paired, low board, my Ten-high with only backdoor straight and flush draws is too weak to continue. Being out of position makes it even harder to float or try to bluff on later streets. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

I am on the button with King-Six suited, which is a playable hand in a late position. No one has entered the pot yet. Raising to 300 is a standard open size in this tournament to put pressure on the blinds and potentially pick up the pot pre-flop or take initiative into the flop.

I am on the Button with an extremely weak unsuited hand (4c 3d). There is no reason to put chips in the pot with this holding. Folding is the only sensible and optimal play.

The hand [8s, 3h] is extremely weak and offers poor playability. As the Small Blind, I am out of position against the Big Blind (Pro). With no incentive to play this hand, folding is the most strategic decision to protect my stack.

My hand, Jack-Six offsuit, is very weak and lacks potential to perform well post-flop, especially out of position against the Big Blind. Flash checked the turn on a board of 9d-Qd-7c-3d. My pair of nines is a marginal hand that has some showdown value, but it is vulnerable to overcards and potential flush draws. I hold a pair of Nines on a board of 9d-Qd-7c-3d-8c. The river card (8c) brought potential straight and flush draw completion possibilities. Queen-deuce suited is a weak hand with poor playability, especially in a three-handed game from the button. There is no incentive to play this hand aggressively or call, so folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to conserve my stack for better spots.

We have an open-ended straight draw with our Jack-Ten on a Qd-8d-2d-9h board. The opponent has checked twice, which typically indicates a lack of a strong made hand or a premium flush. Holding 4-8 offsuit, I have one of the weakest possible starting hands. As the chip leader, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with poor cards in a 3-handed game. Folding is the disciplined play here to wait for a more advantageous position and better equity.
