Facing a 3bb open from the chip leader on the button, flatting would put us in a tricky post-flop situation OOP, and 3-betting as a bluff with this kicker carries unnecessary risk. The most disciplined play is to fold and look for more profitable spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 9c, which is a strong suited connector. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. With 46.5 BBs, I have a comfortable stack to see a flop. I am getting good pot odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 650). 89s plays very well post-flop, and since I am the short stack relative to the others, I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot manageable while realizing my hand's equity.

On a flop of Th-7d-3s, holding 9c-8c gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Jack). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to them and see how they act. My hand has significant equity, so I plan to continue if they bet, but checking allows me to control the pot size while I am drawing.

I have flopped bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a dry, uncoordinated board. Checking back is the optimal play to maintain pot control and realize my equity. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause my opponent to fold hands that I am currently beating, so checking keeps the pot manageable while I assess the turn.

On the turn 6s, I have an open-ended straight draw (9, 8 on a board of 10, 7, 6). Flashlight checked back on the flop, which often indicates a lack of a strong hand or a willingness to go to showdown cheaply with marginal equity. Since this card improves my perceived range as the Big Blind defender and I have significant semi-bluff equity, I will lead out with a bet of 350 (slightly over half-pot). This allows me to take the pot down now against their air/overcards or build a pot for if I hit my straight on the river.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board of Th-7d-3s-6s. Flash, the big blind, led out for 350 into a pot of roughly 650 (prior to his bet). While my pair of 3s is weak, the pot odds are roughly 3:1, justifying a call to see a river. The board texture is somewhat coordinated, so he could be betting with a draw (like 5-4 or 9-8) or a weak made hand that he checked on the flop. Given that I opened the button, I have a wide range and should not fold to a single bet when I have at least a piece of the board, especially since he may be testing me with a bluff after checking the flop.

On the river 6c, the board is Th-7d-3s-6s-6c. I currently hold 9-high, having missed my open-ended straight draw (needed a 5 or a J). I led out on the turn for 350 and was called by Flashlight. This call typically indicates that Flashlight has a piece of the board, such as a 10, a 7, or a mid-pocket pair. Since I am out of position and have no showdown value, I have two options: check-fold or attempt a large bluff. Given that Flashlight called the turn, they are unlikely to fold a 10 or even a weak 7 to a single river bet. As the short stack, I must preserve my remaining 41BBs for better opportunities rather than risking a significant portion of my stack on a low-frequency bluff. I will check and likely fold to any bet.

I am on the river with a pair of threes on a board of Th-7d-3s-6s-6c. My opponent, Flash, has checked, which indicates he either has a weak hand, missed his draws, or is potentially trapping with a slow-played monster. Betting here does not make sense as I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and betting would essentially turn my hand into a bluff. Checking behind allows me to get to showdown for free and win the pot against his air or missed draws, while minimizing losses if he has a better hand.
Q3o is a weak unsuited hand with poor playability and very low equity. Even on the button, this is a clear fold, as it doesn't meet the minimum hand strength requirements for an open raise.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. Pro has folded from the Button. While the cost to call is only 50 more, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when playing out of position against the chip leader, Flashlight. Given my position as the short stack, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand rather than risking chips in a marginal situation.

I am on the button with K6o. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a steal or to play in position. I have approximately 54 big blinds, and while K6o is a marginal hand, the positional advantage justifies a raise to the standard size of 3 big blinds which I have consistently used in previous orbits.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-3 suited, which is a very weak and marginal hand. The Button has opened for a raise. Calling here would put me out of position against an aggressive player and also invite the Big Blind to potentially squeeze or get involved. Given the weak nature of my cards and the poor positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined and strategic play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 65o (Six of Spades, Five of Hearts) facing a 3x open raise from the Button. Offsuit connectors like 65o play poorly post-flop, especially out of position, and do not have enough equity to justify a call here. Folding is the optimal decision.
I am on the button 3-handed with a playable K9o. Opening the pot with a standard raise is a good way to assert position and potentially win the blinds or take initiative into the post-flop play.

KJs is a strong suited broadway hand that plays well. Facing a standard button open from Flashlight, 3-betting from the Small Blind puts pressure on the opener, takes the initiative, and discourages the Big Blind from squeezing or seeing a cheap flop. A raise to 950 is consistent with my previous 3-betting sizes.

I am holding 7c 5s in the Big Blind. There has been an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. This hand is far too weak to defend against a 3-bet, especially out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind after my button open, my K9o is dominated by most of the opponent's 3-betting range. Calling 650 more chips is not profitable with a weak king, as I will be out of position in a bloated pot. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

76o is a weak offsuit connector. As the first to act on the Button, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify opening, especially against two aggressive opponents in the blinds. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button folds, 4h 6h is a decent suited hand to play aggressively. Raising to 300 allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, or set up a continuation bet on the flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c Tc. The Small Blind (Flash) has raised to 300, and the Button (Pro) has folded. The pot currently contains 400 chips (100 from my BB, 50 from SB, and the 250 raise difference). I need to call 200 more to see the flop. 8c Tc is a respectable suited connector that has decent playability post-flop, especially given my chip lead. Defending the Big Blind here is the standard play rather than folding or 3-betting, as I want to keep the pot manageable and utilize my positional advantage post-flop.

On a flop of Ad-3h-3d, I hold 6h 4h. This board is very favorable for the pre-flop aggressor as it heavily favors my perceived range which includes many strong Aces. The paired board also makes it less likely for the Big Blind to have connected significantly. I will lead with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to capitalize on fold equity and represent the Ace.

The flop is extremely dry with an Ace and paired Treys. My Ten-high has zero showdown value and no meaningful draws. Flash raised pre-flop and continued with a bet on the flop, suggesting they likely have an Ace or are representing one. With no equity to justify a call, folding is the correct disciplined play to preserve my stack.
I am on the button with AcTc, which is a strong starting hand in 3-handed play. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

I am in the small blind with 7s4s. This is a weak, non-connected, offsuit-potential hand that plays poorly out of position against a button raise. Given the current stack depths and the positional disadvantage, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

KQo is a strong hand that plays well as a 3-bet from the Big Blind against a Button open. 3-betting allows me to extract value from dominated hands, deny equity to his weaker broadways/suited connectors, and take the betting lead. I'll size up to 950 to put pressure on his moderate stack size.

I opened from the button with Ace-Ten suited, a strong hand in 3-handed play. Pro responded with a 3-bet to 950 from the big blind. While Ace-Ten suited can be vulnerable to larger 3-bet ranges, it is too strong to fold here, especially since I will have position for the remainder of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and use my positional advantage to navigate the rest of the hand.

The flop comes T-5-3 rainbow, a very dry and disconnected texture. Since I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage. A small continuation bet of around 30% of the pot works well here to fold out his missed overcards, Ace-highs, and potentially some smaller pairs. If I face resistance (a call or raise), my King-high has very little equity and I can comfortably give up, but the c-bet is highly profitable at taking down the dead money when he whiffs.

On a board of 3c-Th-5d, I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). The pre-flop action involved me opening from the button and calling a 3-bet from Pro in the big blind. Pro has now led out with a continuation bet of 600 into a pot of 1950. This small sizing (roughly 30% of the pot) is typical for a range-based C-bet in a 3-bet pot. While I am likely ahead of hands like AK, AQ, or smaller pairs, I am behind overpairs like JJ-AA. By calling, I maintain pot control, keep Pro's bluffs in the hand, and utilize my positional advantage to see how the turn develops without overcommitting my stack against a potential overpair.

After my flop c-bet was called, Flash's range is heavily weighted towards pairs (Tx, pocket pairs 66-99) or floating hands. The turn 9s improves some of his floats and doesn't change the board texture enough to make him fold a Ten or a stubborn pocket pair. Since I only have King high with no realistic draws, checking and giving up is the best play. Betting again would unnecessarily bleed chips against an opponent whose SPR indicates they are unlikely to fold a made hand.

On the turn board of 3c-Th-5d-9s, I still hold top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). Pro, who 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, has now checked. This check often indicates either a surrender with overcards like AK or AQ, or a pot-control move with a marginal hand or an overpair like JJ. Since the pot is already quite large (3150) relative to my remaining stack (4200), checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, ensures I see a river card, and keeps Pro's potential bluffs in the hand. Betting here risks a check-raise from a superior hand (like JJ, QQ, KK, or AA) which would force me into a difficult spot for my tournament life. I will check to realize my showdown value.

With King-Queen high and no pair on a very dry board, my absolute hand strength is weak. Flash's check on the turn indicates he likely has a marginal made hand with showdown value, such as a weak Ten, a Nine, a middle pocket pair, or a stubborn Ace-high. Bluffing the river after checking the turn is unlikely to be believable or successful in folding out his pairs. Checking and surrendering the pot (or occasionally winning against a busted float like Queen-Jack) is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

On a board of 3c-Th-5d-9s-2c, I have top pair with an Ace kicker (TPTK). Pro 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop for 600 but has since checked both the turn and the river. While I likely have the best hand, there is little value in betting as most hands that call me (like JJ, QQ, KK, AA) actually have me beaten, and hands that I beat (like AK, AQ) will likely fold. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and realize my equity in a large pot without risking a check-raise.
Jack-five offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, making it a clear fold from the button in this situation. I should wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

With King-Four offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button folding, my hand is too weak to justify a raise or a call. K4o plays poorly post-flop and stealing with it is mathematically unsound against a typical Big Blind defending range. Folding is the safest and most optimal play here.
93o is an extremely weak, disconnected offsuit hand. From the button, folding is the standard and optimal play to conserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with Qs4s, which is a weak hand to defend against an open raise. Playing out of position with a dominated hand like Q4s is not strategically sound. Given the current stack sizes and my position, folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my chip lead.

Holding 8-6 offsuit in the button position 3-handed is a weak hand. Given the stack depths and the positional disadvantage of acting first pre-flop, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to conserve chips.

The 3d introduces a flush draw, but checking protects my range, keeps the pot manageable, and can induce bluffs from missed flop floats or draws. If Flash bets, I still have a comfortable bluff-catcher to call with.

By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and keep Pro's range wider for the river. This is a standard 'check back' spot with middle pair to reach showdown cheaply.

With top pair and a modest kicker on a board where the opponent checked back the turn, their range is likely capped at weak Ax, Qx, or missed draws. A thin value bet of around 40% of the pot on the river is optimal to extract value from those marginal made hands. If the opponent raises, we can safely fold.

I have second pair (Queens) with a 7 kicker. In a blind-vs-blind battle, Pro raised pre-flop and made a small continuation bet on the Ace-high flop, which I called. With ten-high and no draw on a dry king-high board, there is no reason to put chips into the pot. Flashlight's check could indicate weakness, but bluffing here with absolute air is unnecessary when I can simply take a free card. Checking behind is the standard and most optimal play.

The board shows 2c 5h Ks 4h and I hold 9d 6d. With a gutshot straight draw and no pair, I have limited showdown value. I've hit bottom pair on the turn, giving me some marginal showdown value but not enough to confidently bet for value. The board is 2c 5h Ks 4h 4c. I hold 9d 6d. I have nine-high, which has virtually no showdown value. This allows me to reach showdown without risking more chips on a pure bluff with no equity.

Pocket sixes is a solid hand in position on the button. Given the tournament stage and my stack depth, raising to 300 is a standard open to potentially take down the blinds or play a pot with initiative.
74 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the button, the best and only sensible play from the small blind is to fold and preserve my stack.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on a board of 8h-Jc-6c. Flashlight has made a standard continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650. Raising might isolate me against better hands like AJ, KJ, or QJ, while calling provides more flexibility to evaluate the turn based on the next card's impact on the various draws.

I have turned a 9-high flush (9c with the 8h-Jc-6c-4c board). While this is a very strong hand in three-handed play, it is not the nuts as any higher club (A, K, Q, or T) makes a better flush. I have bottom set on a board that now has a completed flush draw and straight possibilities. Since the opponent checked, I need to bet for value and to protect my hand by charging potential draws. On the turn, the four of clubs completes a possible flush. I currently hold the jack of hearts and the nine of clubs, giving me a 9-high flush. I have a 9-high flush on a board where the flush completed on the turn. Flashlight has been aggressive on every street, betting the flop and turn with significant sizing. The river card 2h completed the flush draw on the board (8h-Jc-6c-4c-2h). While my set of sixes is strong, it is now vulnerable to a flush or a 5-7 straight. I am dealt an extremely weak hand (92o) on the Button. This is far below the minimum threshold of hands I would consider opening, even from late position. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3s, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Even though the pot odds are tempting, calling with such a dominated hand against a Small Blind raise is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (Js 2c). I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Small Blind. My hand is 5h 4s, which is an offsuit disconnected low connector. It plays very poorly post-flop and has very low equity. The standard and most profitable decision is to fold this weak holding.

I am on the button with Queen-Eight offsuit, which is a reasonable hand to open with in late position to steal the blinds, given my healthy stack of 14,600 and the need to maintain aggression in this tournament.

Facing a button raise from Flashlight, 96s is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind out of position. Calling invites a squeeze from the big blind and is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the standard play.

I am first to act on the flop after calling from the Big Blind pre-flop. I have flopped trip Queens on a Q-A-Q board, which is a very strong hand. With the opponent checking to me, I need to start building a pot to extract value. A bet of 350 into a 650 pot should entice any Ace or lower pair to continue while allowing me to control the action.

I have 8-high on a board of Qd-As-Qh. I have no pair, no draws, and very little potential to improve. The board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Ax and Qx hands. QJo is a standard and strong opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which is my standard open size, to build the pot and take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d-6h, which is a very weak, uncoordinated hand with poor playability post-flop.
